Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78

INTRODUCTION & CERTAIN DETERMINATIONS1
1. The Applicant (“Garryduff”) seeks certiorari quashing the decision of An Coimisiún Pleanála2 (“the
Commission”) by order3 dated 6 November 2023 to refuse it planning permission for 85 residential units on a
residentially-zoned site at Kilminchy, Portlaoise, Co. Laois (“the Impugned Decision”, “the Proposed
Development” and “the Site”). The refusal was grounded only in the Commission’s view that the proposed
mix of housing types was inconsistent with a development plan objective and hence with proper planning
and sustainable development.
2. I am grateful to all counsel for their helpful submissions and their focussed and practical approach to
the case.
3. Garryduff applied to the Notice Party (“the Council”) on 29 July 2021 for permission for 85 three-
bedroom houses. The Laois County Development Plan 2017-2023 (“the 2017 CDP”) was operative at that
time. I have not seen it, but that is because the parties agree that it did not address housing mix – or at least
did not do so in quantified terms, as did its successor, which applied when the Impugned Decision was made.
The Council sought further information by request dated 22 September 2021 (“RfI”) – including as follows:
“7. Housing Mix
Following discussions with the Housing Department with regards to the mix of unit types, it is noted
that the demand for 3 bedroom social housing units has been satisfied. There is currently a demand
for one and two bedroom units. The applicant is requested to submit their comments and proposals
in this regard.”
This request derived from social housing requirements for which Part V PDA 2000 provides and not from any
other or general development plan content as to housing mix. However, I respectfully disagree with
Garryduff’s submission that the formulation of this request in Part V terms has implications for the decision of
these proceedings. The significance of the RfI is simply that it drew attention to housing mix as an issue and,
more importantly, prompted a reply in particular terms as to housing mix.
1 Headings are not a curial part of this judgment and do not circumscribe the content thereunder. They are provided for navigational assistance only.
2 The Respondent herein - by s.495(3) of the Planning and Development Act 2024, as from 18 June 2025, references to An Bord Pleanála are construed
as references to An Coimisiún Pleanála.
3 ABP-313771-22.
2

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
4. Garryduff’s reply, dated 21 December 2021, to the RfI (“RRfI”) stated as to housing mix:
“The revised layout includes proposals for a block of duplex units consisting of 5 no. 1-bed apartments
and 5 no. 2-bed apartments …The proposals outlined above means that over 11% of the proposed
development will be dedicated to one and two-bedroom units.”4
This increased the number of units proposed from 85 to 89. 10 of those 89 were 1- and 2-bedroom units.5 As
will be seen, the RRfI’s primary significance now is Garryduff’s assertion that over 11% of the proposed units
will be one- and two-bedroom units. I should say that by its use of the word “over” Garryduff does not assert
an open-ended exceedance of 11%: it merely asserts a precise figure slightly exceeding 11%.
5. The Council’s planner6 considered the RRfI at “acceptable” as having “introduced a number of 1 and
2 bedroom apartments into the scheme.” On 27 January 2022, the Council sought further clarification from
Garryduff – but not as to housing mix.
NEW DEVELOPMENT PLAN TAKES EFFECT DURING PLANNING APPLICATION & OBSERVATIONS
6. The next significant occurrence, during the process before the Council, was that the Laois County
Development Plan 2021-2027 (“the 2021 CDP”) took effect on 8 March 2022. By CDP Housing Strategy
Objective HPO17 it provides that applications for residential development of 10 units or more should:
“Ensure that 35% … provides for single and two person households.”
GENERAL OBSERVATIONS
7. I should make some general observations at this point.
• First, HPO1 is in both strong (“ensure”) and quantified (“35%”) terms.
• Second, while Garryduff attempted in these proceedings to argue against a correlation between “1-
and 2-bedroom units” and “single and two person households”, I respectfully reject that argument on an
XJS8 interpretation of HPO1 as if by an intelligent layperson - as opposed to a legalistic interpretation. The
correlation may not be inevitably and invariably precise (no doubt a single person can, and some do, occupy
3-bedroom houses) but it is at least close in general terms and a layperson would regard the concepts as
generally and broadly interchangeable.
4 §7.
5 The Inspector’s second report, of 5 October 2023 §3.1.5 notes that “This was achieved by replacing two pairs of semi-detached houses by a terrace of
10 duplex units and a pair of semi-detached houses by a larger detached 5-bedroom house”.
6 Report 25 January 2022.
7 2021 Development Plan, at Chapter 4. HPO stands for “Housing Policy Objective”.
8 In re XJS Investments Limited [1986] IR 750 - recently confirmed in Sherwin v. An Bord Pleanála [2024] IESC 13.
3

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
• Third, it is necessary to consider the position of a planning permission applicant whose application is
pending on the coming into effect of a new development plan and also to consider the relationship of a
development plan to the reasons which may be given for a planning decision.
8. In that third regard, it is important first to recollect the purpose and status of a development plan. A
development plan is an environmental contract by a planning authority with the community in its functional
area “embodying a promise by the council that it will regulate private development in a manner consistent
with the objectives stated in the plan …” – McGarry.9 It and its effectuation are “answerable to public
confidence”- as “a representation in solemn form, binding on all affected or touched by it, that the planning
authority will discharge its statutory functions strictly in accordance with the published plan” and will do so
“openly and transparently” – Byrne.10 Generally, Browne says that “The development plan lies at the heart of
the planning legislation” and “occupies a pivotal role in the determination of any application for planning
permission”.11 As was said in Ebonwood: “It is of the utmost importance that the public at large and in
particular those persons seeking to develop their lands or property should have certainty and precision as to
the relevant criteria by which any application for permission will be judged ... The first reference point in their
consideration will be the provisions of the development plan.”12 In Element Power, Haughton J described as
the “four corners” within which planning decisions must be made as “the 2000 Act, existing government
policy and objectives, existing ministerial guidelines, and existing county development plans.”13 Though the
Commission can, the planning authority cannot grant permission in material contravention of the plan. It is
also important to recollect that while detailed rules – procedural and substantive – attend the adoption of a
development plan, its general description, stated by s.10(1) PDA 2000, is that a development plan is “an
overall strategy for the proper planning and sustainable development of the area of the development plan.”
In other words, a development plan is necessarily the view of the Planning Authority as to of what, at least in
general terms (and in some respects quite specific terms), the proper planning and sustainable development
of the area consists. Charleton J in Wexele14 approved Browne15 to the effect that in deciding planning
applications on their merits “The terms of the development plan are not conclusive: the overriding
consideration must be the proper planning and sustainable development of the area.” – but also to the effect
that “In practice consideration of the proper planning and sustainable development of an area, and of the
development plan, are often inextricably linked; development objectives may be characterised as an attempt
to articulate in general terms the proper planning and sustainable development of the area.”
9. It follows therefore, and is relevant in this case, that, when considering the reasons given for
planning decisions, a decision affirming conformity, or non-conformity, of a proposed development to
development plan content is, ipso facto, the expression of the decision-maker’s view as to what amounts to
proper planning and sustainable development as it relates to the proposed development in question. Of
course, in a given case, the position may be more complex – a more elaborate reason may be required as to
9 AG (McGarry) v Sligo County Council [1991] 1 IR 99 at p113.
10 Byrne v Fingal County Council [2001] 4 IR 565.
11 Simons on Planning Law, 3rd Ed’n (Browne) §§1-11 & 1-40.
12 Ebonwood Ltd v Meath County Council [2004] 1 I.L.R.M. 305.
13 Element Power Ireland Ltd v An Bord Pleanála [2017] IEHC 550.
14 Wexele v An Bord Pleanála (No. 1) [2010] IEHC 21.
15 Charleton J cited Simons - Planning and Development Law (2nd Ed., 2007) at para. 1.23. I update the reference to Simons on Planning Law, 3rd Ed’n
(Browne) §1-45 & 1-47.
4

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
proper planning and sustainable development, than simply an assertion of conformity or non-conformity of
a proposed development to development plan content - e.g. GOCE.16 The potential complexity is also
demonstrated by the statutory possibility of a permission in material contravention of a development plan –
which is nonetheless considered in accordance with proper planning and sustainable development. But it is
at least often and ceteris paribus a ready inference that, where a decision-maker states that a proposed
development conforms to or contravenes a development plan, that is, per se, an adequate statement
whether the proposed development conforms to or contravenes proper planning and sustainable
development.
10. In that third regard, it is important, next, to observe that in the world of planning, and as planning
relates to the functional area of a planning authority, the coming into effect of a new development plan is a
highly significant event. It invariably involves significant evolution of planning policy for the area in question
– which changes may, often will, have significant implications for the prospects of planning applications.
Every planning advisor must be aware of the significance of a new development plan. Further, it has long
since been taken in practice17 and has been confirmed in Crofton,18 that a planning decision-maker must
decide a planning permission application having regard to the development plan in force at the date of the
decision – not that, if different, in force when the planning application was made and on the basis of which it
was prepared. As Farrell J put it in a Protect East Meath case: “The hazards of making an application at the
cusp of a change of development plan have been highlighted by the issues arising in this case. …….. Timing is
everything, …… Many of the issues and arguments in these proceedings would have been avoided had the
Notice Party made its application more quickly after the Board had issued its Opinion on the pre-application
consultation, or waited until Meath County Development Plan 2021 - 2027 had come into effect.”19
11. In short, a planning permission applicant is on the hazard of the effecting of a new development plan
while the planning application is pending and the applicant must manage that hazard in his/her own
interest. As I said at trial, when the target shifts one must shift one’s aim. In that respect McMonagail,20
though a case about the particular position of quarry operators to whom a particular statutory regime
applies, is illuminatory of what is generally expected of a planning permission applicant:
“The onus is on an applicant making an application under the relevant statutory processes to collate
all relevant material and anticipate the issues which might reasonably arise from that material in
light of the applicant's particular circumstances and the relevant criteria under the statutory scheme.
A quarry owner or operator must be taken to be aware of the potentially very significant
consequences for it of decisions under the statutory processes and it cannot present itself as some
form of passive entity waiting to be informed as to what the decision-maker regards as weak points
in its application so that it can then make submissions or further submissions on those points.
16 Infra.
17 Simons on Planning Law, 3rd Ed’n (Browne) §6-319.
18 Crofton Buildings Management CLG v. An Bord Pleanála [2022] IEHC 704, [2024] IESC 12.
19 Protect East Meath v. An Bord Pleanála & Rockmill [2025] IEHC 276. While the point is generally a good one, it should be said that the difficulties in
that case were particular to the Strategic Housing Development regime prescribed by the Planning and Development (Housing) and Residential
Tenancies Act 2016 which gave limited procedural scope for submissions by planning applicants during the process.
20 McMonagail Stone v Ireland & An Bord Pleanála [2023] IEHC 223, [2023] 4 JIC 2809.
5

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
In my view, this expectation of self-interested pro-activity by planning applicants applies in the case of a
planning applicant, such as Garryduff, whose application is pending when a new development plan takes
effect. It follows that it will almost inevitably be prudent on the part of the planning permission applicant
and his/her planning advisors to consider any implications of the new plan for the prospects of the
application and, if needs be, to seek to make submissions to the planning decision-maker accordingly. “In
such matters, the law helps those who help themselves”.21
12. There is, of course, considerable continuity between successive development plans. Often, the
novelties in a new development plan will represent subtle or minor changes only or changes of marginal
relevance to a particular planning application. It may be that on considering a new plan a planning applicant
will decide, if only on balance or tactically, to leave well enough alone. But generally the applicant takes any
risks of such a choice.
PARTICULAR OBSERVATIONS
13. Here, however, it is not a matter merely of objectionable hindsight to say that the relevant novelty in
the 2021 CDP was not subtle or minor or only marginally relevant to Garryduff’s planning application. It was
the introduction, for the first time, in emphatic terms (“ensure”) of a precisely quantified (“35%”) and
simply-expressed development plan objective as to provision for single- and two- person households in any
residential development of 10 units or more. Garryduff’s application, as revised, was for 89 units. It had itself
advised the Council that only 11% of those would be 1- and 2-bedroom units. Objectively, the application of
HPO1 to the Proposed Development, the 69% shortfall22 and the resultant hazard to the planning application
can only have been obvious – inter alia to Garryduff and its advisors. In fairness, counsel for Garryduff all but
accepted that.23
14. I say “objectively” as, as will be seen, the application of HPO1 to the Proposed Development and the
69% shortfall was not, it seems, subjectively apparent to the Council or, at least at first, to the Board’s
Inspector. However, I am satisfied that, whatever the explanation for their subjective views, the objective
view is what matters here.
15. In this context and as illustrative of the practical significance of HPO1, I recall that counsel for
Garryduff properly accepted24 (as I think he had to) that, if one posited Garryduff’s first making its planning
application after the 2021 CDP had taken effect, it would inevitably – “absolutely” - have had to address in
its planning application HPO1 and its 35% objective. It appears to me that, in the events which in fact
occurred, once the 2021 CDP had taken effect it became inevitable and entirely foreseeable that HPO1
would bear on Garryduff’s pending application and, as to the need to address HPO1, Garryduff was in a
21 Pat O’Donnell & Co v Dublin City Council & Uniphar [2024] IEHC 671 citing Baile Bhruachlain Teoranta v Galway County Council [2024] IEHC 604 in
which Humphreys J cited Benjamin Franklin and Euripides to the effect that the ancient Greek gods took a similar approach.
22 (35-11)/35 x100 = 69.
23 Transcript p17, 11:26.
24 Transcript p15 et seq. See also p36 11:52.
6

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
position little different, if different at all, than it would have been in had it first made its planning application
after the 2021 CDP had taken effect.
16. Objectively, therefore, the prudent course was for Garryduff to respond to the hazard presented by
HPO1. As will be seen, it in fact had opportunity to respond. In fact it did nothing. While its precise response
was for it to decide, it could, for example, have suggested:
• a revised development proposal substituting additional 1- and 2-bedroom units for 3-bedroom units.
• a planning condition to the same effect.
• that the non-compliance was a non-material contravention of HPO1 such that permission should be
granted as conforming to proper planning and sustainable development.
• to the Commission that, despite material contravention of HPO1, permission should be granted as
conforming to proper planning and sustainable development.
17. Also , and while it now complains of the delay inherent in making a new planning application (though
I understand it has made one – I do not know when or in what terms), Garryduff could have made one once
the 2021 CDP came into effect in March 2022 and could have had it in preparation from whatever earlier
point in the CDP adoption process the prospect of HPO1 became apparent. However, that was a matter on
which it was entitled to take its own view and it does not weigh against them in this judgment.
FROM COUNCIL’S CLARIFICATION REQUEST OF 27 JANUARY 2022
18. Garryduff responded on 19 April 2022 to the Council’s clarification request of 27 January 2022. In so
doing it did not address the 2021 CDP or HPO1. Given the events and opportunities for submissions which
followed, I need not decide whether, after the 2021 CDP took effect, Garryduff had opportunity to make
submissions on HPO1 to the Council. The Council decided on 13 May 2022, in terms which did not address
HPO1, to grant permission for the Proposed Development. A third-party appeal to the Commission ensued.
(“the Appeal”). The Appeal raised no issues of housing mix - nor did it cite the 2021 CDP.
19. Garryduff made a submission on the Appeal. It did not address the 2021 CDP or, specifically, HPO1.
That must be regarded as Garryduff’s voluntary choice. It accepts (as it must) that it could have done so.25 It
had an opportunity to do so and didn’t take it.
20. As it happens, Garryduff’s submission on the Appeal was rejected by the Commission as made out of
time. Garryduff accepts that such rejection was legally valid. It seems to me that, as to fair procedures, that
rejection weighs little, if at all, against Garryduff. What does weigh against Garryduff is that it had, but failed
to take, opportunity to make submissions to the Commission as to the bearing of the 2021 CDP, and
specifically HPO1, on the determination of the Appeal.
25 Transcript p19 et seq. Also p28 11:41.
7

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
INSPECTOR’S REPORTS
21. The Commission appointed an Inspector to report on the Appeal – which she did by first report
dated 24 February 2023. It noted the RRfI and the RRfI revision of the Proposed Development to include
duplexes,26 thus increasing the number of units from 85 to 89.27 It identified the 2021 CDP as applicable28 but
did not address it in any detail. Specifically, it did not advert to HPO1. Nor did the Inspector consider housing
mix to be an issue – as it is not mentioned in her first report. The Inspector recommended the grant of
permission on conditions which did not address housing mix or HPO1.
22. The Commission, by direction dated 7 September 2023, requested an addendum Inspector’s report,
by reference to the 2021 CDP, on issues including density, residential amenities and housing unit mix. The
Inspector’s Addendum Report, dated 5 October 2023, whilst not altering the recommendation to grant
permission, found, on the evidence presented, that the Proposed Development did not comply with HPO1 –
stating, inter alia:
“3.3. Mix of Housing Types and Residential Amenities
3.3.1. Policy Objective 1 (PO129) states: ‘ensure that 35% of any residential developments (10 units
or more) provides for single and two person households’. The proposed development provides 11% of
units which are suitable for single and two person households. This does not comply with the
development plan objective. It was however found acceptable by the planning authority.
Replacement of further detached or semi-detached houses, by duplexes similar to those currently
proposed, would not unduly impact on the overall layout and could be conditioned.
…
I am satisfied that the density, and residential amenities, are acceptable in light of the Laois County
Development Plan 2021-2027. As noted above the mix of housing is deficient in its provision for
smaller households.”30
23. The Addendum Report also notes that the issues raised in the Commission direction requesting an
addendum report, including housing type mix, were not raised by the parties to the Appeal and that “should
the Board be minded to take these matters into account in their decision”, s.137 PDA 2000 “may apply”.31
26 Report §3.6.
27 Report §3.7.4.
28 Report §5.2.
29 Sic. In fact HPO1.
30 §3.3.1.
31 § Other issues.
8

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
IMPUGNED DECISION
24. The Commission did take the issue of housing mix/HPO1 into account in its decision. It refused
permission for one reason only, as follows:
“Policy Objective 1 (HPO1) of the [2021 Development Plan] states: “ensure that 35% of any
residential development (10 units or more) provides for single and two person households”.
It is considered that the proposed development which proposes 11% of the 85 units are suitable for
single and two-person households does not comply with this Development Plan Objective. The
proposed development would, therefore, be contrary to the proper planning and sustainable
development of the area.
In deciding not to accept the Inspector’s recommendation to grant permission the … [Commission]
considered the proposed housing mix of 11% of the 85 units for single and two person households
was contrary to Development Plan policy and contrary to proper planning and sustainable
development of the area.”
PLEADINGS - AMENDED GROUNDS, GROUND 2 & SCOPE OF CASE AS ARGUED
25. The Statement of Case32 identified Core Grounds 2 and 3 as impugning the Commission’s compliance
with its duty to give reasons for its decision. In fact, in the Amended Statement of Grounds, Core Grounds 3
and 4 are the “reasons” grounds. Core Ground 2, which I set out below, was not a “reasons” ground.
26. Yet the pleaded Particulars of Core Ground 2 are headed “The impugned decision is invalid in that it
has failed to give sufficient reasons for not accepting the recommendation of the Inspector in arriving at its
decision” and the particulars which follow do not obviously make reasons complaints at all. Indeed, they do
not even use the word “reasons”. That they would use that word is not a strict requirement but the absence
illustrates the disconnect between the core ground as to reasons for not accepting the recommendation of
the Inspector and the particulars proffered in its support. Unhelpfully, the particulars introduce what are in
truth new core grounds - such as regard to irrelevant considerations, prejudgment, bias, irrationality, and
breach of fair procedures. I observe as follows.
• First, while novelty is possible and they do overlap, there is an established taxonomy of core grounds in
judicial review. I have just listed some of the major ones. Where possible – and it all but invariably is
possible - core grounds should identify themselves by reference to that taxonomy and particulars should
confine themselves to elaborating only the core grounds thus identified. Particulars are not an
opportunity for introducing what are in truth new core grounds or for defensive “kitchen sink” or
“scattergun” pleading. Pleadings in judicial review must be precise.
32 Filed pursuant to PD HC126.
9

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
• Second, the disconnect between the core ground as to reasons for not accepting the recommendation of
the Inspector and the particulars proffered in its support created confusion at trial as to what exactly
was the scope of Garryduff’s case in this regard. As was said in a Ballyboden case33 where in a statement
of grounds “there is genuine ‘doubt, ambiguity or confusion’ an Applicant in Judicial Review cannot have
the benefit of it”.
27. Third, as O’Donnell J said in Shatter,34 even objective bias is an ugly allegation which should not be
lightly made and care should be taken to establish that there is a sufficient factual and legal basis to make
the claim before it is made. There was never here any basis for a plea of bias. In fairness, counsel at trial did
not stand over, much less pursue, the plea. But it should never have been pleaded.
28. Fourth, counsel did seek to persist in an allegation of prejudgment on the basis that the Commission
made its decision without first hearing Garryduff.35 While a fair procedures point might properly be made –
breach of the principle audi alteram partem – that is not a prejudgment point. To be clear, whatever it may
mean colloquially (In fairness to counsel, I think he deployed the word colloquially), as a plea in law
prejudgment is a form of bias – see SWI.36 When pleaded or argued at trial, that is its clear connotation.
Even in its objective form, it asserts a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker deliberately both
decided the matter prematurely and shut out a party entitled to be heard. It is as ugly as any other form of
allegation of bias. There is no basis for such an allegation in this case. The allegation should have been
withdrawn and ultimately it was.37
29. The Statement of Grounds provided particulars of only three of the four Core Grounds. It gave no
particulars of what was in fact Core Ground 2. Counsel sought to explain this by saying that Core Ground 2
itself satisfied all pleading and particularity requirements. I respectfully disagree. For example, Core Ground
2 pleaded that the Commission had erred in interpreting HPO1. Clearly, to be pursued, that plea would have
required particulars: first, stating the correct interpretation of HPO1; second stating that which the Applicant
attributed to the Commission; and third contrasting them to demonstrate the asserted error. At trial,
counsel for Garryduff did not pursue, but did not formally abandon, Core Ground 2. Rather, he accepted that
High Court precedent binding me was against him and that he did not argue for a “Worldport”38 departure
by me from that precedent. The implication, as I understood it, is that he sought to keep Ground 2 in the
deep freeze to thaw out if he finds himself elsewhere in due course. I dismiss Core Ground 2 on that basis.
However to avoid confusion, given the re-enumeration of the Core Grounds in the Statement of Case, I make
clear that this dismissal is of Core Ground 2 as pleaded in the Statement of Grounds.
30. I mention that the Statement of Grounds provided particulars of only three of the four Core Grounds
also as explaining why the particulars in the Statement of Grounds of Core Ground 4, as to the Commission’s
33 Ballyboden Tidy Towns Group v. An Bord Pleanála & Ors. [2022] IEHC 7, [2022] 1 JIC 1001.
34 Shatter v Guerin [2021] 2 IR 415.
35 Transcript p99.
36 SWI, IFI, Sweetman & Ors v ALAB et al [2024] IEHC 421 §129 et seq.
37 Transcript p101.
38 Re Worldport Ireland, [2005] IEHC 189, [2005] 9 I.C.L.M.D. 13. In this regard see the transcript p94 & 95.
10

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
reasons for concluding that the Proposed Development contravened HPO1, were provided sub nom Ground
3. Again, they introduced what were in effect, additional core grounds: irrationality, “a premature and
irrelevant and/or improper consideration” and an alleged obligation to “if necessary, impose a condition
which would in their view, satisfy HPO1”. At hearing, correctly and unsurprisingly, counsel for Garryduff
disavowed alleging any such obligation.39
31. Incidentally, also pleaded in this context was a positive plea of “the clear understanding by the
Notice Party that the proposed development in question did not offend the Housing Policy objective HPO1.”
If, as a matter of fact, Garryduff positively had such a “clear understanding”, that plea could readily be
interpreted as an admission by Garryduff, That is as the Commission’s case was that Garryduff should have
appreciated the relevance of HPO1 to the determination of the Appeal and made submissions to the
Commission accordingly and had opportunity to do so. However, as the plea did not feature in submissions
or argument, I will ignore it.
32. Ultimately the case was argued on the fairly simple basis set out in the Statement of Case - as to
contravention of s.137 PDA 2000 and on two fairly discrete reasons issues. As I indicated at trial, I will
consider the case on that basis.40 In fairness to both parties, I think that basis sensibly reflects both the
essence of what Garryduff intended to plead and that which the Commission considered acceptably clear in
the pleadings. There is no pleading difficulty with Ground 1 – breach of s.137 PDA 2000. The Statement of

G1 – DISCUSSION & DECISION
Interpretation & Application of s.137
44. I agree that the caselaw on s.137 does not much assist – though that is no criticism of the cases
given the context in which they considered s.137. It is, however, common case that the animating principle
of s.137 is that of fair procedures. Also, and whatever its substance, it is clear that when the conditions for
its invocation are satisfied, the power s.137 confers on the Commission is not discretionary. The Commission
must exercise it. The s.137 power is in imperative and beguilingly simple terms.
45. It is common case that the 2021 CDP and its HPO1 were, in s.137 terms, matters,
• “other than those raised by the parties or by any person who has made submissions or observations to
the Board in relation to the appeal”.
• Which the Board in determining the appeal or referral took into account”.
The Commission’s resistance to the allegation of breach of s.137, therefore, rests at least in part on the
assertion that the 2021 CDP and its HPO1 were not, in the terms of s.137, matters to which it “may” have
regard but were matters which it “must” have regard.
46. It is well established that, depending on context, the word “may” can be, though it is not usually,
imperative – it can mean “must” or “shall”. The cases were canvassed in Heneghan.49 Here the question is a
bit different – whether “may” includes or excludes “must”. The question arises in a highly complex planning
code in which the Commission must have regard to very many matters – such as s.28 guidelines. But there is
in reality a multiplicity of such documents, on myriad subjects, many of which will be, in truth, more or less
irrelevant to a given planning appeal. In reality, the Commission has appreciable discretion as to whether,
other than a notional way, it has regard to them. Similarly, while the Commission must have regard to a
development plan, much of such a plan will be more or less irrelevant to the appeal in hand. While I do not
so hold, it seems to me that s.137 is at least a context in which a may/must distinction may blur in practice. I
would be cautious of unintended consequences for more complex cases in taking a view on the relatively
simple facts of this case as to the interpretation of s.137 as it relates to that may/must distinction. If
necessary to decide this case I would, of course, be obliged to take a view. But, fortunately I think, it is not so
necessary.
47. On that basis, I assume for present purposes (but do not find) a breach of s.137 as Garryduff alleges.
The question remains whether that assumption justifies certiorari on the facts of this case.

Fair Procedures
48. It is, as stated above, common case that the animating principle of s.137 is that of fair procedures. In
that light, the following considerations appear to me to be relevant.
49. By s.37(1)(b) PDA 2000 it determines appeals as if the application had been made to it in the first
instance - South-West Regional Shopping Centre.50 It is fundamental to the PDA 2000 that the Commission
considers and decides appeals by way of “full de novo assessment of the application” – Alen-Buckley.51 It
does so on the criterion of “proper planning and sustainable development of the area”52 – and to that end
must “have regard” to the development plan.53 It can grant permission for a modified development on foot
of revised plans.54 So, the Commission, in deciding an appeal, is not confined to consideration of only the
issues raised in the appeal – as, indeed, is demonstrated by s.137 PDA 2000, which provides for submissions
by the parties on issues not raised in the appeal.
50. It follows that, in making replying submissions to an appeal, a person such as Garryduff in the
subject Appeal, is not confined to the issues raised in the appeal but may address any issues relevant to the
Board’s consideration of the appeal. Indeed, though a contrary position had been intimated, counsel for
Garryduff, correctly and properly, accepted at trial that this is so.55 S.129 PDA 2000,56 which governs the
right to make submissions and observations in an appeal provides no support for a contrary view. And in
Morehart57 Farrell J found that an observer’s entitlement to make observations in an appeal is “not
restricted” in “nature or scope” – are “not limited in terms of scope or subject matter”. A fortiori, that must
be true of the applicant for permission in responding to a third-party appeal. Undoubtedly, this unrestricted
entitlement to make submissions includes submissions as to the bearing on the determination of the appeal
of the relevant development plan – including a development plan which took effect after the planning
application was made.

Discretionary Relief
55. Inaction by the claimant (in McMonagail terms, passivity), lack of prejudice and proportionality of
relief are factors relevant to the consideration of the exercise of the discretion to refuse relief – see Reilly.62
In the circumstances of this case, the underlying purpose of s.137 – audi alteram partem - was not frustrated
on the facts of this case. Indeed, it was amply met and Garryduff suffered no relevant prejudice. The failure
was Garryduff’s in failing to avail of its opportunity to be heard. In those circumstances, certiorari would be
disproportionate to the breach of s.137 which I have assumed for the sake of argument.
56. Accordingly, and of my discretion, I refuse to grant relief on Ground 1.

LAW AS TO REASONS - S.34(10) PDA 2000 & CASELAW
57. S.34(10) PDA 2000 provides, as relevant, that a decision in a planning appeal “shall state the main
reasons and considerations on which the decision is based”. Where the decision “to grant or to refuse
permission is different, in relation to the granting or refusal of permission, from the recommendation in” the
inspector’s report, the decision “shall indicate the main reasons for not accepting the recommendation in the
report or reports to grant or refuse permission.”
58. The law as to the adequacy of reasons given by administrative decision-makers for impugned
decisions reasons is recently summarised, though explicitly inexhaustively, in RWWA63 and Foran.64 Much of
that summary is irrelevant here. It suffices to note that:
• Adequacy of reasons is fundamental to trust in public administration – Balz65and NECI.66 In similar
vein, Farrell J recently said in GOCE67 that “The obligation to provide reasons is a fundamental requirement of
a valid administrative decision.” The obligation is the administrative law analogue in of the common law’s
requirement that justice should not only be done, but be seen to be done - Number 1 Poultry.68
• The requirement is to provide “the main reasons on the main issues”.69 That requirement is
understood in terms of the “broad issues” in, and “broad gist” of, the impugned decision.70 There is no
obligation to provide a discursive, narrative consideration. Reasons must state the “essential rationale” of
the impugned decision and must “allow the applicant to understand in general terms why the decision was
made” - “why the decision went the way it did”.71 Reasons must disclose that the decision-maker has
“directed its mind adequately to the issue before it”.72 A “reasoned” decision “demonstrating” the
decisionmaker’s reasoning process is required73 - as opposed to merely a statement of the conclusion
reached. The Supreme Court has said that, while the refined logical reasoning and expression of a court
judgment is not required, it is necessary is that, on a broad and common-sense approach, “a party, and in
due course a reviewing court, can genuinely understand the reasoning process.”74 Reasons must, in context,
be “adequate to the situation” considered as a question of substance not form and applying common-sense

60. Some additional observations assist here. Sexton84 is authority that, on differing from its inspector,

61. Grassridge86 cites GOCE as an example of the observation that merely conclusionary reasoning may
well be inadequate. But Grassridge also makes clear that this principle requires careful application - I would
add, application by reference to the requirements, identified above, of a common-sense and practical
approach to the question whether the decision discloses its essential rationale. Grassridge, citing, inter alia,
Duffy87continues: “However”,
• reasons must be read objectively.
• adequacy of reasons is case- and context-specific. They must be “adequate to the situation” as a
matter of fairness and as “a question of substance and not form”. So, “there is a balance to be struck”. I add
that what may look at first blush like an objectionably conclusionary statement may be viewed differently in
its contextual light and when read by the informed reader.
• as reasons need not be given where they are obvious, it follows that in such cases the decision may
be expressed in conclusionary terms.
62. The law does not require “reasons for reasons” or “micro sub-reasons”.88 These are just ways of
saying that what is required are “main” reasons disclosing the “essential rationale” – “what the decision is
fundamentally saying” and “why the decision went the way it did”89 – rather than highly detailed and refined
logical reasoning.
63. Usually, the argument in a reasons case is not as to the substance of the law on reasons but as to its
application to the facts of the particular case. Whether a proffered reason adequately discloses the decision-
maker’s reasoning (as it must) or is merely conclusionary may be a fine, contextual and fact-sensitive
exercise (e.g. O’Neill90) on which differing, indeed plausible and properly semantic, views may be possible.
As to whether a proffered reason is flawed as merely conclusionary, and while in this respect they articulate
approaches, not tests, the cases seem to me to assist as they approve a common-sense and practical
approach, standing back to see what the decision is fundamentally saying and asking whether there is “real
doubt” as to “why the decision went the way it did”.

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
GOCE (2024) ON SIGNIFICANCE OF NON-COMPLIANCE WITH DEVELOPMENT PLAN
64. Garryduff cites GOCE91 as authority that
• mere demonstration of non-compliance with a development plan is, per se, an insufficient reason
for refusal of a planning permission.
• further reasons are required to explain why that non-compliance with the development plan implies
that the development proposal falls short of proper planning and sustainable development – which is, all
agree, the overarching consideration in a planning decision.
65. True in GOCE, Farrell J., in the context and on the facts of that case, considered that a finding of
contravention of the development plan did not suffice to explain why the development proposal fell short of
proper planning and sustainable development. But, in my view, Garryduff pitched the significance of GOCE
too high. It errs in reasoning from the particular of GOCE to the general. I read GOCE as requiring that
reasons for refusal of permission disclose why the development proposal falls short of proper planning and
sustainable development. And in the context and on the facts in GOCE, that required going beyond merely
finding contravention of the development plan. An important context in GOCE, absent here, was the finding
that the Commission had misinterpreted the relevant development plan.92 I do not read GOCE as holding, as
a matter of law, that demonstration of non-compliance with a development plan necessarily cannot, or even
generally does not, suffice as demonstration of inconsistency with proper planning and sustainable
development. It did not suffice in GOCE but may well do so in other instances. Context matters and
common-sense applies. An important context in general is that, as I hope to have demonstrated above, a
development plan’s terms are fundamentally informed by, and are an authoritative (even if not always
decisive93) application and expression by the planning authority of, the concept of proper planning and
sustainable development of the area to which it applies. While, in that regard, the Commission may or may
not agree with the planning authority, it is hardly surprising or mysterious if it does. Even if a reason is not
obvious absent recourse to the development plan’s terms, such recourse may make it so. Or it may not –
depending on context. In short and in my view, properly understood, GOCE
• properly recognised the possibility that, in a given case, a finding of contravention of the
development plan may not suffice to explain why the development proposal fell short of proper planning
and sustainable development.
• did not hold that in all cases a finding of contravention of the development plan will not suffice as
explaining why the development proposal fell short of proper planning and sustainable development.
66. As I observed at trial,94 to correctly say, as GOCE in effect does, that as to a proposed development a
development plan is not always the last word on the issue of proper planning and sustainable development
and further exposition of that issue may be required is not, logically, to say it can never be the last word on
particular facts. Nor for that matter, is it even to say that it is not often or even usually the last word.

GROUND 395 - REASONS WHY PROPOSED DEVELOPMENT DID NOT SATISFY OBJECTIVE HPO1
67. The Statement of Case addressed together what it enumerated as Grounds 2 and 3. However it
seems to me clearer to address them separately. I will consider Ground 3 first.
GARRYDUFF’S POSITION
68. The Commission concluded that only 11% of the units in the Proposed Development would be suited
to single- and two-person households, against a HPO1 objective to “ensure” 35%. Garryduff asserts that
• the Inspector based her conclusion that the Proposed Development of 11% of units for single- and
two-person households does not comply with HPO1 and that the housing mix “is deficient in its provision for
smaller households” not on the suitability of the units for single- or two- person households, but on an
unreasoned quantitative exercise where only the outcome was detailed. She did not record on what
evidence she relied and/or how the HPO1 criteria were not met.
• the Commission provided “no reasoning” and “no mathematical methodology” by reference to
evaluative criteria in the 2021 CDP, and breached its duty under s.34(10) PDA 2000 to provide its own
reasons for concluding that only 11% of the units met the criteria for single- and two- person households;
• due to the deficiency in the reasons provided it is unable to challenge the methodology of
application of HPO1. It professes to be “at a loss” as to the basis for the 11% figure and to be “in the dark” as
to how the decision was made.
COMMISSION’S POSITION
69. The Commission says the reasons for refusal in this case are obvious. HPO1 straightforwardly
requires 35% of the units to cater for single and two-person households and only 11% do so. Garryduff itself
provided the 11% figure to the Council. Like the numerically quantified objective at issue in Clane
Community Council,96 HPO1 is “… simple, straightforward and mathematical, and doesn’t confer any
planning judgement…”. Garryduff “can have no real doubt as to why it lost” - as to what the Commission’s
reason were. In this regard it cites Leefield97 and Killegland.98

G3 - DECISION
70. The Commission’s submissions convince. As has been seen, the given reason for the Impugned
Decision asserted that the Proposed Development did not comply with CDP Housing Policy Objective HPO1
to “ensure that 35% of any residential development (10 units or more) provides for single and two person
households” as 11% of the proposed development units are suitable for single- and two-person households.
71. Given argument at trial, it is necessary to observe that the plea here is not
• of reasons inadequate to explain a finding that the Proposed Development is inconsistent with
proper planning and sustainable development.
• that the finding that the Proposed Development did not comply with CDP Housing Policy Objective
HPO1 does not supply a reason adequate to explain a finding that the Proposed Development is inconsistent
with proper planning and sustainable development.
Those observations distinguish the case from GOCE. Here we have, in the end, a simpler and narrower plea
which does not raise wider issues of proper planning and sustainable development. The plea is simply of
inadequate reasons why the Proposed Development did not comply with HPO1. The strict rules of pleading
in judicial review confine Garryduff to that plea.
72. To the extent that its particulars elaborate the Core Ground, Garryduff pleads that the Commission
“never recorded or undertook any or any adequate analysis of the units that were to be built”. As best I can
discern, this assertion appears to be based in a view that, whereas Garryduff’s own RRfI asserts that over
11% of the proposed units will be one- and two-bedroom units, that assertion did not of itself permit of a
conclusion that 11% of the Proposed Development units are suitable for single- and two-person households.
Garryduff’s proposition appears to be that the phrases “one- and two-bedroom units” and “single- and two-
person households” do not coincide. I have above rejected that view, considered in context, as linguistic
cavilling of a kind in which the XJS interpreter would not indulge. In my view, such a reader, on a common-
sense, practical and objective view, is left in no real doubt as to why, in this respect, the Impugned Decision
went the way it did.
73. Accordingly, I respectfully reject Ground 3 as to reasons for concluding that the Proposed
Development did not satisfy HPO1.

GROUND 299 - FAILURE TO GIVE REASONS FOR NOT ACCEPTING THE INSPECTOR’S RECOMMENDATION
GIVEN REASON & INITIAL OBSERVATIONS
74. As has been seen, the Impugned Decision states, that
“In deciding not to accept the Inspector’s recommendation to grant permission the … [Commission]
considered the proposed housing mix of 11% of the 85 units for single and two person households
was contrary to Development Plan policy and contrary to proper planning and sustainable
development of the area.”
75. I agree with Garryduff that this reason does not appreciably elaborate on – it essentially repeats –
the Commission’s general reason for refusing permission. However, it would be a logical fallacy to suggest,
on this account alone, that the Impugned Decision fails the duty to explain non-acceptance of the Inspector’s
recommendation. The point is not to compare the general reason with the reason for non-acceptance of the
Inspector’s recommendation: the point is to test the reason for non-acceptance of the Inspector’s
recommendation against the duty to give reasons for such non-acceptance. Depending on circumstance, a
given reason may satisfy both duties. And, as recorded above, Leefield is authority that in some
circumstances, on a straightforward question the reason may be terse where there is “really little more to be
said.”
WHAT DID THE INSPECTOR RECOMMEND?
76. The law does not prescribe the form of an inspector’s report. Yet anyone who has read more than a
few knows that, though departures are permissible, in practice and more or less consistently (at least in
relatively simple files, not involving complex EU Law issues, such as this was), they adhere to a template. The
contents list (a very helpful element of any such report) of the Inspector’s first report reveals it to have been
in conventional form. It reads, in part,100 as follows:
• 1.0 Site Location and Description
• 2.0 Proposed Development
• 3.0 Planning Authority Decision
• 4.0 Planning History
• 5.0 Policy Context
o 5.6. EIA Screening
• 6.0 The Appeal

o 7.2. Appropriate Assessment
o 7.3. Principle of Development

77. It is important to say both that headings in Inspector’s reports are not determinative as to subject-
matter or status of content and that such reports must be read as a whole – not as isolated chapters.
Nonetheless, headings are informative and it is readily to be assumed that the form of such reports is
informed, as relevant here, by the duty imposed on Inspectors by s.146(2) PDA 2000 to make
recommendations in their reports.
78. Here, the Inspector’s first report, as has been noted, mentioned the 2021 CDP as applicable but said
no more of it and, in particular, did not address HPO1. It contained the following:
“8.0 Recommendation
8.1.1. In accordance with the foregoing I recommend that the proposed development be permitted,
for the following reasons and considerations, in accordance with the following conditions.”
79. There follow two sections which are clearly to be read as recommended content of the
Commission’s decision.
“8.0 Reasons and Considerations
The proposed development, in an area zoned for residential development, is part of the Kilminchy
development impacted by servicing capacity constraints, which are being or are to be addressed by
the local authority. The proposed development would benefit from these works and should contribute
to the exceptional costs. With the benefit of the remedial works the proposed development would be
in accordance with the proper planning and sustainable development of the area.”
80. §10.0 lists recommended conditions. Condition 1 is the usual condition requiring development in
accordance with the plans and particulars supplied by Garryduff. They include the RRfI dated 21 December
2021, which included a revised proposal of 89 units, of which over 11% would be one and two-bedroom
units in a block of duplexes. Otherwise, no recommended condition addresses housing mix or the provision
of units for single- and two-person households.
81. In response to the Board’s direction of 7 September 2023 to, inter alia, further consider the mix of
housing types in light of the 2021 CDP, the Inspector’s second report, in terms set out above,102 observed
that

• the Proposed Development of 11% of units for single- and two-person households does not comply

• The Council had found this acceptable.

82. Given the first report had noted that the 2021 CDP applied and could have addressed the terms of
the 2021 CDP but did not do so, and the obvious disparity between the 35% requirement of HPO1 and the
11% provision in the Proposed Development as revised by the RRfI, I think the Commission’s direction of 7
September 2023 must be read as an assertion of the inadequacy of the first report – including by reason of
its failure to address HPO1.104 I must assume that the Inspector, in writing her second report, appreciated
that as the position and yet remained conscious of her statutory duty to include a recommendation in her
report. Given s.146 and s.37(10)105 PDA 2000, certainty and clarity of an inspector’s recommendation is
important. Also, it is not difficult to infer that while a recommendation must be read as part of an Inspector’s
report as a whole, it is at very least desirable from the point of view of legal certainty and likely is required
by s.146 PDA 2000, to explicitly identify and express the recommendation of an inspector’s report. Indeed
that is in fact the all-but-invariable practice of the Commission’s inspectors, I am conscious of the need in the
present case to interpret both Inspector’s reports together as a whole, while also reading the second report
as a specific response to the Commission’s direction of 7 September 2023. While, taking her reports as a
whole, it must be considered that she let her recommendation in her first report stand, it would have been
helpful had she, in her second report, explicitly addressed the issue of recommendation.
83. As has been seen, in her second report, the Inspector’s positing the application of s.137 was framed
in terms of having drawn the Commission’s attention to the possibility of addressing replacement of further
houses by duplexes. It uses the following phrase of this and of her consideration of the other issues raised in
the Commission’s direction of 7 September 2023: “should the Board be minded to take these matters into
account in their decision”. However, the Inspector does not explicitly recommend replacement of further
houses by duplexes. Nor does she explicitly alter – or, for that matter, even mention - the recommendation
of her first report. Her second report contains no explicit recommendation. Nor does her second report
propose or recommend the terms of a condition providing for the replacement of further houses by
duplexes.
84. Accordingly and not without hesitation, I have concluded as a matter of interpretation of her reports
that the Inspector did not, explicitly or implicitly, recommend replacement of further houses by duplexes in
order to approach or meet the 35% requirement of HPO1. I draw that conclusion in circumstances in which
she had not, in her first report, recommended such a condition and on the basis that, had the Inspector in
her second report intended to alter the explicit recommendation of her first report by recommending a

she would have done so explicitly as a recommendation and, most likely, would have provided the terms of

Proposed Development acceptable in her view (which might or might not have fully reached 35%). Instead,
she identified the possibility as available “should the Board be minded” to pursue the issue.
85. The Commission was not substantively obliged to avoid refusing permission by imposing a condition
requiring replacement of further houses by duplexes in order to comply with HPO1. While Garryduff did
ventilate that proposition, it was ultimately not pursued at trial. Nor, ordinarily, was the Commission obliged
to provide a reason for not imposing such a condition. For present purposes, what matters is that it follows
from the interpretation which I have ascribed to the Inspector’s reports that the Commission was not
required by s.146 PDA 2000 to provide specific reasons for not imposing a condition requiring replacement
of further houses by duplexes in order to comply with HPO1 - as no such condition had been recommended
by the Inspector.
86. However as it did reject the Inspector’s recommendation, in the terms stated in her first report, to
grant permission, the Commission bore in that regard to the duty as to reasons imposed by s.34(10)(b).
G3 – FURTHER DISCUSSION & DECISION
87. Perhaps due to the amalgamation of their submissions on the two reasons grounds and perhaps due
to an erroneous view that, in her second report, the inspector had recommended a condition aimed at
satisfying HPO1, once I find that she did not do so, the parties’ remaining written submissions on the issue of
reasons for not accepting her recommendation become a little sparse. Garryduff says the Commission failed
to engage with the Inspector’s rationale for her conclusions in her first report that the Proposed
Development was consistent with the proper planning and sustainable development of the area. The
Commission says that its citation, as its reason for disagreeing with the Inspector’s recommendation that
permission be granted, of non-compliance with HPO1 as to housing mix sufficed to express and explain that
disagreement as to planning and sustainable development. It says that it believed by reason of non-
compliance with HPO1 that a grant of permission was not justified and that is clear and lawful reason for
rejecting the Inspector’s recommendation.
88. Garryduff’s submissions on this issue understandably stress GOCE and Sexton as to the enhanced
duty to give a reason for disagreeing with the inspector’s recommendation. On other facts such arguments
might prevail. However, on present facts, they are overwrought. This is, in the end and on its particular facts,
a simple case. A common-sense understanding of the Commission’s reasons, on a straightforward down-to-
earth reading, leaves no room for real or genuine, as opposed to forensic, doubt as to what the Commission
decided vis-à-vis the Inspector’s recommendation and why. This case is in the Leefield category of cases in
which the reason given for rejecting the Inspector’s recommendation was terse on a straightforward (for
that matter, simply quantifiable) issue and there was really little more to be said.

Garryduff Properties v ACP [2026] IEHC 78
89. That said, I will yield to the temptation of saying a little more: not to suggest that it should have
been said, but simply to illustrate the obvious:
• First as I have said, the Development Plan, specifically here the 2021 CDP and HPO1, is an
authoritative expression of the Council’s view of the proper planning and sustainable development of its
area. There can be no doubt but that the Commission was entitled to accept that view and that its
acceptance could likewise, without more and at least in this instance, be understood as an authoritative
expression of its view of the proper planning and sustainable development of the Site.
• Second, HPO1 is expressed in emphatic (“ensure”) and simply quantified (“35%”) terms. So there is a
strong impetus to apply it and it is relatively straightforward to apply. It is equally straightforward to discern
on-compliance. Or, at least, it was so in this case.
• Third, the Proposed Development fell very considerably short of compliance with HPO1: by 69% of
the required units for single and two person households - that is, by 24%106 of the total number of units.
• Fourth, on the issue of compliance with HPO1, there was in truth nothing in the Inspector’s
recommendation with which the Commission, in its reason for rejecting her recommendation, could have
“engaged”. The Inspector simply had not addressed HPO1 in her recommendation and, inasmuch as she
addressed HPO1 in her second report she did not make a recommendation in that regard.
• Fifth, and perhaps unnecessarily, I point to the bald statement of the Inspector in her second report:
“the mix of housing is deficient in its provision for smaller households”.
90. In my view, the Commission’s given reason for rejecting its Inspector’s recommendation sufficed in
law and I reject Garryduff’s assertion to the contrary.
REASONS FOR REFUSAL ILLUMINATORY OF PROSPECTS OF FUTURE PLANNING APPLICATION
91. Garryduff’s written submissions asserted that it was entitled by the Commission’s reasons “to know
how the decision-making process was conducted in respect of its proposal such that it would assist a future
planning application in the event it was non-compliant.” This was not a pleaded complaint and is dismissed
on that account.
92. However, lest I am wrong to dismiss it as not pleaded I will decide the issue. It is an issue on which
there is conflicting authority.107 Weston108 states that the requirement for reasons arises in order, inter alia,
“to indicate on what ground an application is refused in order that it can become known as to whether it is
appropriate to make a further application or not”. However no authority is cited in Weston and it is not
106 35 – 11 = 24.
107 See generally, Simons on Planning Law, 3rd Ed’n (Browne) §§3.128 & 13.373-5.
108 Weston v An Bord Pleanála [2010] IEHC 255.

apparent that this assertion was part of the ratio of that decision. Garryduff cites South Bucks DC109 for the
same proposition. But, unlike here, that was a case of a statutory provision which required that the court
must be satisfied also that “the interests of the applicant have been substantially prejudiced” by the failure
to give adequate reasons110 and in that context considered that the interest of an objector could be
prejudiced by a decision to grant permission for reasons which failed “to indicate what, if any, impact they
may have in relation to the decision of future applications.” I confess that I would have thought that
prejudice to the objector as to hypothetical future applications may often be of little weight compared to
the prejudice of not being told why one has lost the present battle. There may not be other battles. In any
event, South Bucks DC does not bind here. Conversely, in West Wood Club111 the court was clearly
expressing ratio in professing itself
“….. satisfied, having regard to the above and to the jurisprudence on the duty to give reasons as set
out in paragraphs 55-59 that the law in this jurisdiction does not go so far as to impose a duty on the
respondent to cite what may be acceptable to it in a future application in order to comply with its
duty to give reasons. ….. Sufficient reasons must be given so as to equip the applicant to appeal the
decision or to judicially review it. …… For the reasons above, I must refuse the relief sought on this
ground.”
93. Were it necessary, I would prefer West Wood Club to the contrary authorities. No doubt, reasons
will often in practice be used to inform the terms of, and will tend to illuminate the prospects of, future
planning applications. Indeed, that may have been all that Weston was intended to convey. However, that
prospect of their utility, which is generally beneficial, does not of itself imply a legal obligation on the
Commission to actively provide reasons which will serve that function. The Commission’s proper concern is
to tell those interested the reasons for its present decision - not to provide hostages to fortune potentially
affecting future decisionmakers as to development proposals not yet formulated. I see little attraction in the
prospect of judicial review of reasons on the specific basis of alleged failure to provide such illumination –
not least as difficult questions would arise, in my view to no good purpose, as to the degree and accuracy of
the illumination required and how that degree and accuracy might be assessed in advance of the
determination of a later planning application. Further, a judgment upholding reasons as providing the
necessary illumination might ultimately be proved illusory by a later planning decision. And, clearly, no
future decision-maker could be bound by such indications as might have been given in an earlier refusal of
planning permission.
94. All that said, Garryduff did not develop its submission in this this regard and I will leave a conclusive
view to a case in which the issue is pursued. In the present case, it suffices to say that the submission fails on
the facts. The Commission’s reasons clearly conveyed that, ceteris paribus, the prospects of residential
permission on the site would likely incrementally improve as the percentage of single- and two-person
households proposed in any future development proposal neared and reached the HPO1 objective to
“ensure” 35%. In truth, that is a proposition so obvious that Garryduff did not need it illuminated.

Buckinghamshire DC v. Porter.
